planting.note: Plant divisions in late summer to early fall in acidic, humus-rich woodland soil with established mycorrhizal networks; pyrola is extremely difficult to establish and transplants should be moved with as much native soil and fungal associations intact as possible.

directions: Plant divisions in late summer in deep shade beneath conifers or mixed woodland, in acidic, humus-rich soil that already supports mycorrhizal fungi; pyrola is obligately mycorrhizal and will fail in any site lacking established fungal networks. Pyrola rotundifolia produces a low rosette of glossy, rounded evergreen leaves that persist through winter, then sends up a slender spike 10-18 inches tall in summer bearing waxy, fragrant white bell-shaped flowers closely resembling lily-of-the-valley. This native wildflower of cool North American and Eurasian forests is one of the most challenging woodland plants to establish and is best acquired from a reputable native plant nursery rather than wild-collected.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in dry to average, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; plantain-leaf pussytoes tolerates poor, rocky soil and is ideal for naturalizing on dry slopes or beneath high-canopied trees where little else will grow.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in dry, poor to average, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; plantain-leaf pussytoes is exceptionally tolerant of drought, rocky substrates, and the dense dry shade beneath high-branched deciduous trees. Antennaria plantaginifolia forms dense mats of silvery-gray, woolly foliage that spreads steadily by stolons to create a weed-suppressing groundcover, bearing clusters of tiny white button-like flower heads in early spring that attract native bees before most other plants are in bloom. This dioecious native of eastern North American open woodlands and rocky hillsides thrives where moisture and fertility are too low for conventional groundcovers, making it invaluable for difficult dry-shade situations.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in dry to moderately moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; solitary pussytoes is well suited to dry woodland understory conditions in the southeastern US where summer heat and shade combine to eliminate many groundcover options.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in dry to moderately moist, well-drained soil beneath deciduous or mixed woodland in partial to full shade; solitary pussytoes is one of the best low-growing native groundcovers for the difficult dry shade of southeastern hardwood forest understories. Antennaria solitaria is distinguished from other pussytoes by producing only a single flower head per stem rather than the clustered heads typical of the genus, a characteristic reflected in both its common and scientific names. It spreads reliably by stolons to form a fine-textured, silvery-green mat that suppresses weeds and provides early-season forage for native bees in woodlands, slopes, and shaded areas throughout the Southeast.

planting.note: Plant transplants in fall in moist, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; woolly sunbonnets is adapted to the longleaf pine savanna and flatwood conditions of the southeastern coastal plain and performs best with periodic disturbance or open canopy that mimics its native fire-maintained habitat.

directions: Plant transplants in fall in moist, well-drained sandy or loamy soil in partial shade to full sun, choosing a site that approximates the open longleaf pine flatwood conditions of its native range in the southeastern coastal plain. Chaptalia tomentosa produces a flat basal rosette of leaves that are deep green on top and strikingly white-woolly on the undersides, giving the plant year-round textural interest even out of bloom. The unusual daisy-like flowers appear from winter through spring, nodding in bud like a sunbonnet before opening to reveal white to pale lavender rays, making this native wildflower a valuable cool-season bloomer in mild southeastern gardens.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped transplants in spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in full shade to partial sun; hobblebush requires consistently moist conditions and cool summer temperatures and performs poorly in sites with hot, dry summers or direct afternoon sun.

directions: Plant in spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in full to partial shade, allowing generous spacing for the broad, arching form; hobblebush requires cool summer temperatures and consistent moisture, and is best suited to northern gardens or high-elevation sites where summer heat is moderate. Viburnum lantanoides produces large, heart-shaped leaves on low-arching branches that root where they contact the ground — the source of the name 'hobblebush,' as the rooted stems can trip unwary woodland walkers — and bears flat-topped clusters of showy white flowers in April and May followed by berries that ripen from red to black and attract thrushes, cedar waxwings, and other wildlife. Fall foliage color is among the most dramatic of any native shrub, turning rich crimson to claret-red in September, making hobblebush a multi-season standout for shaded northern woodland gardens.
